claims.claim: Bumiputera procurement was built for B40 Malays.
claims.source: Eleventh Malaysia Plan Strategy Paper 3 states that government procurement and contracts are instruments to develop Bumiputera entrepreneurs and should be restructured to select capable, qualified and genuine Bumiputera entrepreneurs. It does not say the procurement system was built specifically for B40 Malays. https://ekonomi.gov.my/sites/default/files/2021-05/Strategy%20Paper%2003.pdf
claims.correction: Replace with: 'Bumiputera procurement preference is intended to support Bumiputera enterprise development, but official planning documents also acknowledge risks of rent-seeking and spurious-front activities. It is not accurately described as a programme built specifically for B40 Malays.'
claims.stale_check: No recent change found that would make the original wording accurate.

claims.claim: The data shows Bumiputera procurement mostly reaches middlemen and T20 boards.
claims.source: No primary source found verifying 'mostly reaches middlemen and T20 boards'. Official planning documents acknowledge rent-seeking and spurious-front risks but do not quantify that most benefits flow to middlemen or T20 boards. https://ekonomi.gov.my/sites/default/files/2021-05/Strategy%20Paper%2003.pdf
claims.correction: Use a narrower claim: 'Official policy documents acknowledge risks of rent-seeking and fronting in Bumiputera procurement, but the scale and income-class distribution require primary evidence.'
claims.stale_check: No recent official update found supporting the stronger claim.

claims.claim: There are RM12B in contract rent-seeking, RM680M in absentee board fees, and race-blanket discounts that ignore income.
claims.source: The RM12B and RM680M figures could not be verified against primary sources. Bumiputera property discounts based on Bumiputera status are discussed in property-sector sources, but the RM4.8B claimed cost was not verified. https://www.iproperty.com.my/guides/bumi-lot-check-status-reason-different-bumi-quota-73067 ; https://thannees.com/demystifying-bumiputera-discounts-paid-to-state-govt/
claims.correction: Separate the claims. State only the property-discount mechanism if sourced, and remove the RM12B and RM680M figures unless primary evidence is supplied.
claims.stale_check: No recent update found validating the RM12B or RM680M figures.

claims.claim: Federal government procurement totals roughly RM150 billion annually.
claims.source: C4 Center's procurement report states that Malaysia spends more than RM150 billion every year procuring goods, works and services, citing David Seth Jones/IDEAS. This is not a direct Treasury figure, but it is a traceable procurement-governance source. https://c4center.org/wp-content/uploads/c4_position_paper_-_procurement_as_good_governance_-_web.pdf
claims.correction: Qualify as: 'One procurement-governance report estimates public procurement at more than RM150B annually.'
claims.stale_check: The figure is older and should be refreshed against current Treasury procurement data before publication.

claims.claim: 30% of federal government procurement, or RM45 billion, is reserved for Bumiputera-owned businesses.
claims.source: MoF materials and the C4 report support a 30% set-aside for certain works or construction-related procurement, not a blanket 30% of all federal procurement. https://www.mof.gov.my/portal/pdf/TPPA/01-taklimat-penambahbaikan-PP-PK-meningkatkan-tadbir-urus-perolehan-kerajaan.pdf
claims.correction: Replace with: 'Certain government works or construction procurement allocations include Bumiputera set-asides or preference margins. It is not verified that 30% of all RM150B federal procurement is reserved.'
claims.stale_check: No recent source found validating the RM45B extrapolation.

claims.claim: RM12B a year leaks from the Bumiputera contract quota to Ali Baba middlemen.
claims.source: Searches of MACC/SPRM sources and broader web results did not locate a primary MACC 2025 source for an RM12B annual Ali Baba leak figure.
claims.correction: Remove the RM12B figure unless the specific MACC report, speech, investigation paper or parliamentary answer is produced.
claims.stale_check: No later update found. The issue is unsupported, not merely stale.

claims.claim: The RM12B leak equals 27% of the RM45B Bumiputera allocation.
claims.source: The arithmetic is correct if the inputs are accepted: 12/45 = 26.67%. However, both RM12B and the RM45B Bumiputera allocation extrapolation are not verified against primary sources.
claims.correction: Do not publish the 27% claim until the RM12B and RM45B inputs are independently verified.
claims.stale_check: No update found validating the inputs.

claims.claim: MACC's 2025 finding was that about RM12 billion of that allocation leaks to Ali Baba arrangements.
claims.source: No MACC/SPRM primary publication was found for this stated 2025 finding.
claims.correction: State as unverified unless the exact MACC publication, date, speaker and document can be cited.
claims.stale_check: No evidence of a later correction or update was found.

claims.claim: Bumiputera quota holders sell contracts to non-Bumiputera operators for 5-15% commissions.
claims.source: The Ali Baba/fronting mechanism is recognised in official policy language as a risk of 'spurious-front activities', but the 5-15% commission range was not verified from a primary source.
claims.correction: Use: 'Official policy documents acknowledge spurious-front activities in procurement. The claimed 5-15% commission range remains unverified.'
claims.stale_check: No recent source found validating the commission range.

claims.claim: 34% of contractors surveyed admitted participating in Ali Baba arrangements.
claims.source: No primary survey, MACC publication, academic paper or official dataset was found verifying the 34% figure.
claims.correction: Remove unless the survey name, sample size, fieldwork date, methodology and publisher can be cited.
claims.stale_check: No update found.

claims.claim: MACC prosecuted 42 Ali Baba cases in 2025.
claims.source: Searches did not locate a MACC/SPRM primary source confirming 42 prosecutions in 2025 for Ali Baba cases.
claims.correction: Replace with: 'No primary MACC prosecution count was located for 2025.'
claims.stale_check: No later MACC update found validating the figure.

claims.claim: There are an estimated 8,000-12,000 active Ali Baba arrangements nationwide.
claims.source: No primary source found for the 8,000-12,000 estimate.
claims.correction: Remove the numerical estimate unless a primary source or defensible methodology is supplied.
claims.stale_check: No update found.

claims.claim: 342 political appointees across 48 GLCs and statutory bodies earned a combined RM680 million in 2025.
claims.source: The closest current governance source found is IDEAS/Pantau Kuasa, which reported 238 political appointments to FSBs and GLCs under the Madani administration, and examined 135 FSBs. It does not support 342 appointees, 48 bodies, or RM680M in fees. https://www.ideas.org.my/publications/policy-ideas-no-88-who-sits-at-the-table-from-political-to-public-appointees-in-malaysias-federal-statutory-bodies-fsbs/
claims.correction: Replace with a sourced governance claim, for example: 'IDEAS/Pantau Kuasa reported 238 political appointments to FSBs and GLCs under the Madani administration.' Do not use RM680M unless source-level remuneration data exists.
claims.stale_check: The claim appears contradicted by a newer 2026 IDEAS/Pantau Kuasa figure, though scope may differ.

claims.claim: The average is RM1.99 million per political appointee.
claims.source: The arithmetic follows from RM680M divided by 342, but the RM680M and 342-person inputs were not verified.
claims.correction: Remove unless the underlying RM680M and 342-person dataset is published and auditable.
claims.stale_check: No update found validating the inputs.

claims.claim: 48 individuals hold 3 or more concurrent board seats, with some sitting on 5 GLCs simultaneously.
claims.source: No primary or credible dataset found verifying the count of 48 individuals or the claim that some sit on 5 GLCs simultaneously.
claims.correction: Replace with dataset-specific language if Pantau Kuasa or company annual reports support named cases.
claims.stale_check: No update found.

claims.claim: GLC board appointees average 4 hours of actual monthly meeting time per board.
claims.source: No primary source found.
claims.correction: Remove or convert into a clearly-labelled assumption with methodology.
claims.stale_check: No update found.

claims.claim: 87% of political appointees lack professional qualification in the industry their GLC operates in.
claims.source: No primary source found for the 87% figure. IDEAS reported that 78% of chairperson roles in FSBs lack defined qualification standards, which is materially different from saying 87% of appointees lack qualifications. https://www.ideas.org.my/publications/policy-ideas-no-88-who-sits-at-the-table-from-political-to-public-appointees-in-malaysias-federal-statutory-bodies-fsbs/
claims.correction: Replace with: 'IDEAS reported that 78% of chairperson roles in the FSBs examined lack defined qualification standards.'
claims.stale_check: The available 2026 source suggests the original 87% claim may be based on a different or unsupported metric.

claims.claim: Board fees grew 34% since 2022 while worker wages in the same GLCs grew 4.8%.
claims.source: No primary dataset, annual-report compilation, or official labour source was found supporting the 34% versus 4.8% comparison.
claims.correction: Remove unless a reproducible dataset of GLC annual reports and wage data is provided.
claims.stale_check: No update found.

claims.claim: The GLC board structure was created in part to develop Bumiputera enterprise leadership.
claims.source: Official development documents discuss Bumiputera enterprise development and GLC/GLIC roles in supporting Bumiputera SMEs, but the specific historical claim about the purpose of GLC board structures was not verified. https://ekonomi.gov.my/sites/default/files/2021-05/Strategy%20Paper%2003.pdf
claims.correction: Use narrower wording: 'GLCs and GLICs have been used as instruments in Bumiputera economic development policy.'
claims.stale_check: No recent change found.

claims.claim: Intra-Bumiputera wealth inequality, Gini 0.39, is greater than inter-ethnic inequality, Gini 0.34.
claims.source: No UBS or Credit Suisse 2025 source was found reporting Malaysia ethnic wealth Gini figures of 0.39 and 0.34. DOSM 2024 instead reports income Gini by ethnic group: Bumiputera 0.369, Chinese 0.412, Indians 0.380, Others 0.352, and national Gini 0.390. https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/income-inequality-malaysia-2024
claims.correction: Replace with verified DOSM income data: 'In 2024, DOSM reported national gross-income Gini of 0.390; by ethnic group, Chinese households had the highest gross-income Gini at 0.412, followed by Indians 0.380, Bumiputera 0.369 and Others 0.352.' Do not label this as wealth Gini.
claims.stale_check: DOSM's 2024 release, published 8 Oct 2025, is more recent and contradicts the claim as written.

claims.claim: T4A issue 1662 cited the Credit Suisse Global Wealth Report 2025.
claims.source: The current Global Wealth Report 2025 is published by UBS. Credit Suisse was acquired by UBS, and the report is branded UBS in the available source. https://www.ubs.com/global/en/wealthmanagement/insights/global-wealth-report.html
claims.correction: Use 'UBS Global Wealth Report 2025' if referring to the current report, and verify whether the report actually contains the claimed Malaysia ethnic wealth Gini data.
claims.stale_check: The Credit Suisse branding appears stale after UBS's acquisition of Credit Suisse.

claims.claim: Race-based policy addresses the smaller distribution because intra-Bumiputera inequality is larger than inter-ethnic inequality.
claims.source: The premise was not verified and is contradicted by DOSM 2024 income data, which reports ethnic income gaps and ethnic-group Gini values but not the claimed inter-ethnic wealth Gini of 0.34. https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/income-inequality-malaysia-2024
claims.correction: Convert into analysis only after using verified DOSM income data or a verified wealth-distribution dataset. Do not present the conclusion as established fact.
claims.stale_check: DOSM 2024 release is the latest official source found and should supersede the unsupported wealth-Gini framing.

claims.claim: The mandatory 5-15% Bumiputera property discount is not means-tested.
claims.source: Property-sector sources state that Bumi discounts usually range from 5% to 15%, while another tax/property source states common discounts of 7% for commercial units and 10% for residential units. These sources describe eligibility by Bumiputera lot/status, not income. However, a primary state-by-state legal source was not found in this pass. https://www.iproperty.com.my/guides/bumi-lot-check-status-reason-different-bumi-quota-73067
claims.correction: Qualify as: 'Bumiputera property discounts commonly operate by Bumiputera status and vary by state; no income means-test was identified in the sources reviewed.'
claims.stale_check: State policies vary and may change. Verify the relevant state rules before publication.

claims.claim: A T20 Bumiputera household receives the same property discount as a B40 Bumiputera household.
claims.source: This is a reasonable inference if the discount applies by Bumiputera status and no income test applies, but no primary state rule was reviewed here proving this universally across Malaysia. Property sources state discounts vary by state.
claims.correction: State as an inference, not a verified national fact.
claims.stale_check: No recent state-level update checked.

claims.claim: Non-Bumiputera buyers cross-subsidise the Bumiputera property discount through an estimated RM4.8 billion in higher prices in 2024.
claims.source: No primary source found for the RM4.8B 2024 cross-subsidy estimate.
claims.correction: Remove the RM4.8B figure unless the calculation, transaction base, state mix, discount rate assumptions and source dataset are disclosed.
claims.stale_check: No update found.

claims.claim: The Malay grievance isn't that Malays lose to other races; it is that programmes built for B40 Malays are captured by Malay elites.
claims.source: This is interpretive, not directly verifiable. It relies on unverified quantitative claims about RM12B leakage, RM680M GLC fees and wealth-Gini comparisons. Official sources do support concerns over rent-seeking and spurious-front activities, but not the full framing.
claims.correction: Reframe as interpretation: 'A defensible concern is that some Bumiputera-targeted instruments may be captured by intermediaries or higher-income beneficiaries, but the scale requires stronger primary evidence.'
claims.stale_check: No recent source found that verifies the framing as a factual claim.

claims.claim: Track Bumiputera contracts to actual workforce, not nominal ownership.
claims.source: Eleventh Malaysia Plan Strategy Paper 3 states that a condition would be imposed on selected entrepreneurs to employ at least 60% Bumiputera employees, supporting the idea that policy design can look beyond nominal ownership.
claims.correction: This is a policy recommendation, not a disputed factual claim. It can be retained if presented as recommendation.
claims.stale_check: The cited planning paper is older; check whether the 60% workforce condition remains current.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Bumiputera procurement was built for B40 Malays.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Bumiputera also includes non-Malay indigenous groups; narrowing it to Malays is inaccurate.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Eleventh Malaysia Plan Strategy Paper 3, EPU.

cardinal_sin_flags.why: 30% applies to construction works specifically, not all federal procurement.
IDEAS reports 238 not 342; RM680M not verified.
IDEAS reports 78% of chairperson roles lack defined qualification standards, different metric.
UBS report doesn't have this; DOSM 2024 income data shows different figures.

omitted_facts: Eleventh Malaysia Plan Strategy Paper 3 acknowledges rent-seeking and spurious-front activities in Bumiputera procurement but does not quantify RM12B leakage.
MoF procurement material distinguishes CPTPP/non-CPTPP procurement and describes Bumiputera preferences by category and value band — more nuanced than blanket 30%.
C4 Center cites older procurement-loss estimates up to RM10B annually, not RM12B specifically from Ali Baba.
IDEAS/Pantau Kuasa: 238 political appointments under Madani; 78% of chairperson roles lack defined qualification standards.
DOSM Income Inequality Malaysia 2024: national gross-income Gini 0.390; ethnic-group Gini Bumi 0.369, Chinese 0.412, Indians 0.380, Others 0.352.
Global Wealth Report 2025 is UBS-branded, not Credit Suisse-branded.
Bumiputera property discounts vary by state under State Authority jurisdiction.
source_assessment: The original Stage 1 analysis is heavily circular, citing T4A's own prior issues as the main evidentiary base rather than primary sources. The public sources found support broad concerns about Bumi procurement preferences, rent-seeking risk, political appointments and property-discount mechanisms, but they do not support the most load-bearing numbers (RM12B leakage, 34% contractor participation, 42 prosecutions, 8,000-12,000 arrangements, 342 appointees, RM680M fees, 87% lack of qualifications, 34% board-fee growth, 4.8% worker-wage growth, RM4.8B property cross-subsidy, the claimed wealth-Gini comparison).
overall_assessment: The analysis is not publication-safe in its current form. Its broad thesis may be arguable as commentary, but the factual scaffolding is weak because most headline statistics are unverified or contradicted by closer sources. The strongest defensible parts are narrower: official documents acknowledge rent-seeking and spurious-front risks in Bumiputera procurement; procurement policy contains Bumiputera preferences and set-asides for specific categories; political appointments in FSBs/GLCs remain a governance concern; and Bumiputera property discounts commonly operate by status rather than income. The RM12B, RM680M and wealth-Gini claims should be removed or replaced unless primary sources are produced.
